Name: spectral_stability_dynamic_compute
Title: Spectral Stability Bounds for Dynamic Compute Allocation in Latency-Sensitive Inference
Short Hypothesis: The spectral stability of hidden states, quantified by their local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate, can serve as a reliable proxy for dynamic compute allocation during inference, enabling strict latency budgets without compromising accuracy.
Related Work: Existing work on training-free inference optimization (e.g., Spectral Quantization Routing) focuses on precision allocation or interference cancellation but does not explicitly link spectral stability to dynamic compute allocation. This proposal distinguishes itself by introducing a formal PAC-Bayes framework that connects spectral stability to inference efficiency, enabling precise, latency-aware compute throttling.
Abstract: We propose a novel framework for dynamic compute allocation during inference that leverages the spectral stability of hidden states. By quantifying the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states, we derive a PAC-Bayes bound that links spectral stability to inference efficiency. This allows us to dynamically route tokens through fewer layers or prune attention heads when stability exceeds a calibrated threshold, enforcing strict latency budgets. We evaluate our method on single-GPU setups across adversarial benchmarks and natural domain shifts, demonstrating a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier. Our approach provides a mathematically transparent pathway for resource-efficient inference under latency constraints.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Measure spectral stability metrics (local covariance condition number, spectral decay rate) across hidden states during inference on diverse benchmarks (e.g., WILDS, adversarial ICL tasks).
Experiment 2: Implement dynamic compute allocation based on spectral stability thresholds, evaluating its impact on inference latency and accuracy.
Experiment 3: Compare the proposed method against static and heuristic baselines (e.g., fixed-layer skipping, attention pruning) on throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers.
Experiment 4: Validate the PAC-Bayes bound linking spectral stability to generalization under perturbation, ensuring theoretical soundness.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed method assumes that spectral stability metrics can be computed efficiently during inference, which may introduce overhead.
The approach may not generalize well to tasks where hidden state geometry is less informative for compute allocation.
The PAC-Bayes bound relies on assumptions about perturbation noise, which may not hold in all practical scenarios.
